skill.name: opentargets-database
skill.description: Query the Open Targets Platform GraphQL API for target, disease, drug, evidence, tractability, safety, and association data used in therapeutic target discovery.
skill.summary: Explore Open Targets evidence for drug target discovery and prioritization.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many high-severity alerts, but semantic review found they are false positives from Markdown code fences, GraphQL field names, and biomedical terms. The real risk is limited to an intentional Python script that sends user query terms to the public Open Targets GraphQL API; no prompt injection, credential handling, command execution, or data exfiltration was found.

low_findings.title: Intentional Public API Network Access
low_findings.description: The helper script posts GraphQL queries to the documented Open Targets endpoint. This is expected for the skill, uses HTTPS and a timeout, and does not include credentials, but users should know it sends query terms to an external public service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script clearly defines the Open Targets GraphQL URL and sends requests.post calls to it. No evidence of credential collection or unrelated endpoints was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Examples Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command detections are fenced Python examples, inline field names, and Markdown reference text. No subprocess, shell, os.system, eval, or exec usage was found in the executable script.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are documentation examples or backticked identifiers, not executable shell calls. A semantic review of the script found no command execution API.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Biomedical Terms Flagged as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts are caused by words such as hash, score, and genetic association in biomedical documentation and GraphQL fields. No cryptographic function or hashing library use was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed contexts discuss evidence scoring and GraphQL response fields. There is no observed MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak cryptographic operation in executable code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Intentional Public API Network Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper script posts GraphQL queries to the documented Open Targets endpoint. This is expected for the skill, uses HTTPS and a timeout, and does not include credentials, but users should know it sends query terms to an external public service.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The script clearly defines the Open Targets GraphQL URL and sends requests.post calls to it. No evidence of credential collection or unrelated endpoints was found.

content.user_title: Query Open Targets Drug Discovery Data
content.value_statement: Drug discovery teams need fast access to target, disease, evidence, and drug data. This skill provides workflows and a Python helper for querying Open Targets through Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
actual_capabilities: Search Open Targets entities by target, disease, or drug name.
Retrieve target annotations, tractability, safety liabilities, and genetic constraint data.
Collect target-disease evidence by Ensembl ID, EFO ID, and evidence type.
List known drugs for a disease with targets, mechanisms, phases, and trial status.
Guide interpretation of evidence types, association scores, and target annotation signals.
Provide reusable GraphQL query patterns for custom Open Targets analysis.
limitations: Requires network access to the public Open Targets GraphQL API.
Does not authenticate, store credentials, or access private biomedical datasets.
Association scores support prioritization but do not predict clinical success.
Large systematic analyses should use Open Targets downloads or BigQuery instead of repeated API calls.

use_cases.title: Prioritize Targets for a Disease
use_cases.description: Find disease-associated targets, compare evidence types, and review tractability and safety signals.

use_cases.title: Validate a Candidate Gene
use_cases.description: Review Open Targets annotations, known drugs, genetic constraint, and supporting evidence for one gene.

use_cases.title: Explore Repurposing Opportunities
use_cases.description: Identify drugs connected to a disease and compare mechanisms, targets, and clinical phases.

prompt_templates.title: Find Entity Identifiers
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Open Targets skill to find the Ensembl target ID for BRCA1 and the EFO disease ID for Alzheimer disease.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize Target Evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Query Open Targets for target ENSG00000157764 and summarize tractability, safety liabilities, genetic constraint, and top associated diseases.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Disease Targets
prompt_templates.prompt: For EFO_0000249, retrieve the top associated targets and rank them using genetic evidence, known drugs, safety, and tractability.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Custom Evidence Query
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a custom Open Targets GraphQL query for a target-disease pair that returns genetic association, known drug, literature, and pathway evidence.

output_examples.input: Find evidence for BRAF in melanoma.
output: Identifies the BRAF Ensembl target record and melanoma disease ontology record.
Summarizes association strength by evidence type, with emphasis on known drugs and somatic mutation evidence.
Highlights tractability, clinical precedence, and safety considerations for follow-up review.

output_examples.input: Which drugs are known for Alzheimer disease in Open Targets?
output: Returns a concise list of known drug records grouped by mechanism and target.
Includes clinical phase and indication status when available.
Notes that repurposing decisions require manual clinical and literature validation.

output_examples.input: Assess a target for safety and druggability.
output: Reviews tractability categories across therapeutic modalities.
Summarizes safety liabilities, tissue expression, and genetic constraint signals.
Separates strong validation signals from caution flags.

best_practices: Start with exact identifiers, then confirm entity names before interpreting results.
Compare multiple evidence types instead of relying on one association score.
Use API queries for exploration and data downloads for bulk analysis.
anti_patterns: Treating Open Targets scores as direct probabilities of clinical success.
Using text-mining evidence alone for high-stakes target decisions.
Sending large batch workloads through the exploratory GraphQL API.

faq.question: Does this skill require an Open Targets account?
faq.answer: No. The documented GraphQL API is public and does not require authentication.

faq.question: Can it run custom GraphQL queries?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes API guidance and a helper function for executing custom GraphQL queries.

faq.question: Does it provide medical advice?
faq.answer: No. It supports research workflows and requires expert interpretation before any clinical decision.

faq.question: What identifiers does it use?
faq.answer: It uses Ensembl gene IDs for targets, EFO IDs for diseases, and ChEMBL IDs for drugs.

faq.question: Is it suitable for bulk analysis?
faq.answer: Only for exploratory work. Use Open Targets downloads or BigQuery for large systematic analyses.

faq.question: What data leaves my environment?
faq.answer: Queries sent through the helper script go to the public Open Targets GraphQL API endpoint.
